summary:
The Croatian War of Independence (1991–1995) began when Croatian Serb militias, backed by the Yugoslav People's Army, resisted Croatia's declaration of independence from Yugoslavia in June 1991. Serb forces seized approximately one-third of Croatian territory and established the self-declared Republic of Serbian Krajina, ethnically cleansing the region of its Croat and other non-Serb populations. The siege and partial destruction of the city of Vukovar in the winter of 1991, and the massacre of prisoners at Ovčara, became symbols of Serb war crimes.

Croatia rebuilt its military with covert US assistance and training, and in August 1995 launched Operation Storm — the largest European military offensive since World War II — which retook nearly all Serbian Krajina territory within days, causing approximately 150,000 to 200,000 ethnic Serbs to flee in what Serb authorities have called a reverse ethnic cleansing. The [[event:3473|Dayton Agreement]] later resolved the remaining Serb-held territory in eastern Croatia (Eastern Slavonia) through a peaceful reintegration process. The Croatian war was part of the broader [[event:2696|Yugoslav Wars]] triggered by the dissolution of Yugoslavia.
